title: Physio Review Timeframes and Follow-up Triggers
synonyms: Physio Review Timeframes and Follow-up Triggers

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline sets practical expectations for when physio reviews should occur and what changes should trigger earlier reassessment or escalation.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- New injury or new physio care plan
- Stable follow-up
- Slow progress or recurrent flare
- Work-capacity reassessment

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Review timing should reflect risk, not habit
- Remote and shift-work settings may justify shorter review intervals because support can disappear quickly
- Failure to improve on the expected trajectory is itself a review trigger

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Review sooner when pain is escalating, weight-bearing is worsening, neurological symptoms appear, sleep is collapsing, or the patient cannot perform basic tasks safely
- For low-risk strains or minor overload, review can be spaced if the patient has a clear self-management plan
- Escalate immediately if new red flags emerge between appointments

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Use clear review intervals linked to function goals, work restrictions, and symptom irritability
- Shorten follow-up when the diagnosis is uncertain, the environment is high risk, or there is no improvement after initial management
- Extend review spacing only when the patient is improving and knows when to re-contact

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - A missed or delayed review in remote practice can matter more than in town because backup care may be hours away
